original_hypothesis.title: Changing transferAllowedContract Away From MergeTgt Permanently Locks All TITN Claims
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt is deployed as the transferAllowedContract on Arbitrum (per arbitrumSetup.ts). On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), ALL transfers are blocked except for exempted addresses. MergeTgt relies on being the transferAllowedContract to transfer TITN to users in claimTitn() and withdrawRemainingTitn(). If the owner calls setTransferAllowedContract() to change it to a different address (e.g., a staking contract or DEX), MergeTgt loses its exemption. All safeTransfer calls in MergeTgt would revert due to transfer restrictions, permanently locking user claims. Additionally, MergeTgt is marked as isBridgedTokenHolder (TITN was bridged to it via sendToArb.ts), making the restriction apply on any chain.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a tight coupling between MergeTgt's claim functionality and Titn's transfer restriction system. There is only ONE transferAllowedContract slot. Changing it is a plausible admin action (e.g., to allow a staking contract or DEX). The consequence â€” permanently bricking all MergeTgt claims â€” is non-obvious and severe. The only recovery is calling setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false), which disables ALL transfer restrictions globally.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts line: await titn.setTransferAllowedContract(deploymentMergeTgt.address) confirms MergeTgt is set as transferAllowedContract. sendToArb.ts bridges TITN to MergeTgt address, marking it as isBridgedTokenHolder.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Single transferAllowedContract slot. MergeTgt relies on being this address to transfer TITN. On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), block.chainid == arbitrumChainId is always true, so ALL non-exempt transfers revert. If transferAllowedContract changes, MergeTgt's from-address is no longer exempt.

preconditions: Owner calls setTransferAllowedContract to a different address
Users have unclaimed TITN in MergeTgt

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only ONE transferAllowedContract slot; MergeTgt is tightly coupled to this being set to its own address
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin changes transferAllowedContract â†’ MergeTgt safeTransfer reverts â†’ all claims locked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit â€” all user claims permanently locked. Admin must disable all transfer restrictions to recover.

economic_questions: Can setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false) fully recover claim functionality?
What is the impact of globally disabling transfer restrictions as a fix?
Is there any plan to change transferAllowedContract during the 360-day period?
validation_requirements: Test claimTitn after changing transferAllowedContract away from MergeTgt
Verify MergeTgt is marked as isBridgedTokenHolder after TITN bridge
Confirm the only recovery is setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A â€” admin action triggers the issue
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit â€” DoS on all claims

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All MergeTgt users with unclaimed TITN

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate transferAllowedContract change and verify claimTitn reverts

validation_result.impact_summary: Changing the single `transferAllowedContract` to support a new integration (e.g., DEX) revokes `MergeTgt`'s exemption, causing all user claims to revert.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `Titn` token implements transfer restrictions that are active by default (`isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = true`). On Arbitrum (`chainId == 42161`), these restrictions apply to all non-owner transfers. To allow the `MergeTgt` contract to distribute claimed TITN to users, it is set as the `transferAllowedContract` during deployment (as seen in `arbitrumSetup.ts`).

However, `Titn` only supports a single `transferAllowedContract` slot. If the protocol needs to whitelist another contract (such as a DEX pair or a staking contract), the admin must call `setTransferAllowedContract(newAddress)`. Doing so overwrites the `MergeTgt` address.

Once `MergeTgt` is no longer the `transferAllowedContract`, any calls to `claimTitn` or `withdrawRemainingTitn` will attempt to transfer TITN from `MergeTgt` to the user. The `_validateTransfer` hook will evaluate to true for the revert condition because:
1. `from` (MergeTgt) is not the owner.
2. `from` is not `transferAllowedContract`.
3. `to` (user) is not `transferAllowedContract`.
4. `isBridgedTokensTransferLocked` is true.
5. `block.chainid == 42161` is true.
6. `to` is not `lzEndpoint`.

This causes the `safeTransfer` to revert with `BridgedTokensTransferLocked()`, effectively DoSing all user claims. To support both the new integration and the claim contract simultaneously, the admin is forced to globally disable transfer restrictions by setting `isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false`, defeating the purpose of the restriction system.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The `Titn.sol` contract defines `transferAllowedContract` as a single `address` rather than a `mapping(address => bool)`. The `_validateTransfer` function strictly checks `from != transferAllowedContract && to != transferAllowedContract`. This single-slot design inherently forces a mutually exclusive choice between whitelisting `MergeTgt` and any other protocol contract, leading to a denial of service for whichever contract is excluded.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The transfer validation logic relies on a single `transferAllowedContract`. If this is changed to another address, the previous allowed contract (MergeTgt) will fail this check and all its transfers will revert.
This function overwrites the single allowed contract slot, meaning only one contract can be exempt from transfer restrictions at any given time.
This transfer will revert if MergeTgt is no longer the `transferAllowedContract`, locking the user's claimable TITN.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When the admin updates `transferAllowedContract`, `MergeTgt` loses its exemption. Subsequent calls to `claimTitn` or `withdrawRemainingTitn` trigger `titn.safeTransfer`, which invokes `_validateTransfer`. Since `MergeTgt` is no longer the allowed contract and the chain is Arbitrum, the transfer is blocked and reverts.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The `arbitrumSetup.ts` script confirms that `MergeTgt` is initially set as the `transferAllowedContract` (`await titn.setTransferAllowedContract(deploymentMergeTgt.address)`).

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An admin decides to whitelist a DEX pair or staking contract to allow trading/staking of TITN. They call `setTransferAllowedContract(dexAddress)`. Immediately, all users attempting to claim their converted TITN from `MergeTgt` experience transaction reverts. The admin realizes the issue and must either brick the DEX by changing the allowed contract back to `MergeTgt`, or globally disable all transfer restrictions by calling `setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false)`.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High impact on protocol availability and admin flexibility. The tight coupling and single-slot design force the protocol into a state where it cannot securely integrate with other contracts without either breaking its core claim functionality or completely abandoning its transfer restriction security model.
recommended_mitigations: Change `transferAllowedContract` from a single `address` to a `mapping(address => bool) public transferAllowedContracts`.
Update `setTransferAllowedContract` to take an address and a boolean to enable/disable specific contracts.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy Titn and MergeTgt on an Arbitrum fork (chainId 42161).
2. Set `transferAllowedContract` to MergeTgt.
3. Deposit TGT and generate claimable TITN for a user.
4. Change `transferAllowedContract` to a dummy address (e.g., a DEX).
5. Attempt to call `claimTitn` as the user and observe the `BridgedTokensTransferLocked` revert.
uncertainties: It is unclear if the protocol intends to whitelist any other contracts during the 360-day claim period.
additional_testing_needed: Test the behavior of `withdrawRemainingTitn` under the same conditions to confirm it is also blocked.
